critic_raw:
The experimental design for CNN-based anomaly detection on IoT time-series data exhibits a fundamental split strategy flaw. The use of a stratified random split on temporal sensor data is inappropriate: IoT sensor readings carry strong autocorrelation across time, and random shuffling allows the model to train on data from time periods interleaved with the test window, inflating generalization estimates. A chronological split (or walk-forward validation) is required so the model is only evaluated on data collected after all training data.

The false positive rate is chosen as the primary metric, but this is misaligned with the anomaly detection framing. In highly imbalanced anomaly detection settings with very few failure events, aggregate FPR can be gamed by a near-constant negative predictor. Precision-Recall AUC, F1 on the anomaly class, or AUCPR would better reflect discriminative power over rare failure events. Reporting FPR alone without reference to recall makes the metric nearly uninformative for the stakeholder goal.

The CNN architecture choice is framed as capturing 'spatial hierarchies in time-series data,' but this rationale is weak: CNNs on 1D sequences capture local temporal patterns, not spatial hierarchies (which require genuine spatial structure). More importantly, the design does not address how the CNN handles variable-length sequences or missing sensor readings, which are common in real manufacturing IoT streams. The baseline (threshold-based system) is tuned to match complexity, but thresholds are often non-parametric heuristics; specifying how tuning was operationalized is necessary to assess fairness.

Confound control for seasonal operational variations is mentioned but not specified mechanistically. Asserting controls without describing the method (e.g., time-blocked cross-validation, including season as a covariate, or holdout periods spanning full seasonal cycles) leaves this as an unverified claim. The interaction between hourly aggregation and the CNN's receptive field also needs justification: if failures are precipitated by patterns at finer granularity, aggregation at hourly intervals may destroy the signal the CNN is meant to exploit.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on autocorrelated time-series sensor data risks temporal leakage; a chronological split is required
False positive rate as primary metric is insufficient for rare-event anomaly detection without paired recall or AUCPR
CNN rationale cites 'spatial hierarchies' incorrectly for 1D temporal data; variable-length and missing-data handling is unspecified
Baseline tuning procedure for the threshold system is not operationalized, making fairness of comparison unverifiable
Seasonal confound controls are asserted but not mechanistically specified, leaving that claim unsubstantiated
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on autocorrelated time-series sensor data risks temporal leakage; a chronological split is required
False positive rate as primary metric is insufficient for rare-event anomaly detection without paired recall or AUCPR
CNN rationale cites 'spatial hierarchies' incorrectly for 1D temporal data; variable-length and missing-data handling is unspecified
Baseline tuning procedure for the threshold system is not operationalized, making fairness of comparison unverifiable
Seasonal confound controls are asserted but not mechanistically specified, leaving that claim unsubstantiated

critic_raw:
The transformer fine-tuning design for radiology report disease code classification is generally well-structured, but several issues require attention. The stratified random split on 10,000 reports is a reasonable choice given the multi-label nature if temporal drift in coding practices is truly negligible. However, radiology reports often reflect evolving departmental protocols and ICD coding revisions over time; a random split can allow training on reports from later time periods to implicitly inform test-time decoding of earlier coding conventions through shared lexical patterns, particularly if the annotators revised historical codes in the same batch process. A temporal split or at minimum temporal analysis of label drift should be documented.

The baseline—keyword-based rule extraction—is described as being tuned 'with equivalent effort for fairness.' However, rule-based systems and neural fine-tuning are qualitatively different in tuning surface area: the former involves lexicon curation and threshold selection, while the latter involves learning rate, batch size, layer freezing, and class weighting. Claiming equivalence by effort is not operationally meaningful. The fairness of this comparison would benefit from a more precise definition of what was tuned and how much compute was allocated.

Macro-F1 as the primary metric is appropriate given label imbalance, but 10,000 reports may be insufficient for reliable Macro-F1 estimation across rare disease codes where some classes may have fewer than 50 positive examples. Small-sample label performance can swing substantially across random seeds, making reported differences difficult to interpret. The design should report confidence intervals or cross-validation variance for Macro-F1.

Departmental confound controls are mentioned (analyzing performance across departments), but the design does not address whether the train/test split respects departmental boundaries. If one department's reports are overrepresented in test, and the model was fine-tuned on reports from another department, performance may be misleadingly poor or good due to domain shift rather than genuine model quality.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores potential temporal drift in coding practices and ICD revision cycles that affect label consistency
Claiming 'equivalent tuning effort' between rule-based and transformer systems is operationally undefined and potentially misleading
Macro-F1 with only 10,000 reports may yield unreliable estimates for rare disease codes with very few positive examples
Departmental confound analysis does not specify whether train/test split respects departmental boundaries, risking domain shift confounds
No mention of handling multi-label correlation structure or threshold optimization per label for the transformer classifier
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split ignores potential temporal drift in coding practices and ICD revision cycles that affect label consistency
Claiming 'equivalent tuning effort' between rule-based and transformer systems is operationally undefined and potentially misleading
Macro-F1 with only 10,000 reports may yield unreliable estimates for rare disease codes with very few positive examples
Departmental confound analysis does not specify whether train/test split respects departmental boundaries, risking domain shift confounds
No mention of handling multi-label correlation structure or threshold optimization per label for the transformer classifier

critic_raw:
The hierarchical attention summarization experiment has a structural conflict at its core: the primary evaluation metric is human scoring on clarity and informativeness, while model selection is performed using ROUGE on the validation set. ROUGE and human judgment of clarity/informativeness are known to be weakly correlated. Selecting a model to maximize ROUGE and then claiming superiority on human scores is not valid without demonstrating that the ROUGE-selected checkpoint also maximizes human preference. This creates a misalignment between the selection criterion and the reported outcome.

The stratified random split across academic domains is defensible if domain membership is stable and the domains span both training and test sets proportionally. However, the design assumes existing abstracts serve as 'natural benchmarks' for generative outputs. This is problematic: author-written abstracts reflect stylistic and rhetorical norms that vary by field, by era, and by author. Using the same abstract as both training target and evaluation gold standard conflates the model's ability to replicate style with genuine informativeness improvement. The evaluation of 'relevance' is particularly vulnerable to this conflation.

Human evaluation protocol is described as using evaluators with 'diverse backgrounds' following 'standardized evaluation parameters,' but no inter-annotator agreement measure is reported or planned. Without Fleiss' kappa, Krippendorff's alpha, or similar, the reliability of the human judgments is unknown. Variability in human assessment is acknowledged as a confounding factor but no concrete mitigation beyond 'standardized parameters' is specified.

The BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR secondary metrics are computed against existing abstracts. For a generative model that produces novel but accurate summaries, these metrics systematically underestimate quality. Relying on n-gram overlap against a single reference is especially weak for summarization of long academic documents with many valid summary paths.
all_issues_raised: Model selection on ROUGE but reporting on human clarity/informativeness scores creates criterion-outcome misalignment
Using author-written abstracts as gold standard conflates stylistic reproduction with genuine relevance improvement
No inter-annotator agreement (IAA) measure planned or reported for the primary human evaluation
BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR against a single reference systematically underestimates quality for generative summarization
Stratified split by academic domain does not address temporal drift in paper styles or citation norms across the arXiv/PubMed collection period
all_issues_adjudicated: Model selection on ROUGE but reporting on human clarity/informativeness scores creates criterion-outcome misalignment
Using author-written abstracts as gold standard conflates stylistic reproduction with genuine relevance improvement
No inter-annotator agreement (IAA) measure planned or reported for the primary human evaluation
BLEU/ROUGE/METEOR against a single reference systematically underestimates quality for generative summarization
Stratified split by academic domain does not address temporal drift in paper styles or citation norms across the arXiv/PubMed collection period

critic_raw:
The dynamic pricing experiment for ride-sharing contains a critical structural problem: the deployment context is online and real-time, yet the experimental design uses a stratified random split on historical observational data. In ride-sharing, prices are endogenous to demand—surge pricing affects how many drivers and riders participate, which in turn affects the observed revenue signals in the dataset. Training and evaluating a pricing model on logged observational data without accounting for this feedback loop (i.e., the counterfactual problem) means MSE on historical revenue is not a valid proxy for the causal effect of the new pricing policy on future revenue.

MSE on revenue predictions is chosen as the primary metric, which measures how well the model predicts recorded historical revenue—not whether the pricing policy would increase revenue in deployment. The stakeholder goal (maximize revenue) requires a causal or policy evaluation framework, such as A/B testing, off-policy evaluation with importance weighting, or simulation. Optimizing predictive MSE on observational data can select models that simply replicate the historical pricing strategy.

The baseline is linear regression on 'historical averages,' which is substantially weaker than the gradient boosting model in feature richness and capacity. This is not a fair comparison: the linear regression baseline is not given access to real-time demand forecasting features, while the proposed model is. The experiment is testing two things simultaneously—model complexity and feature access—making the attribution of any improvement ambiguous.

Driver availability is listed as a confound to be controlled by inclusion as a covariate. However, driver availability is itself a downstream consequence of pricing decisions, making it a mediator rather than a true confounder. Including mediators as covariates blocks the causal pathway being studied and can bias estimates of the pricing model's effect on revenue.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split ignores endogeneity: historical prices affect observed demand and revenue, violating the i.i.d. assumption needed for observational evaluation
MSE on historical revenue predictions does not measure whether the dynamic pricing policy improves future revenue causally
Baseline (linear regression on historical averages) lacks access to real-time demand features, confounding model capacity with feature access
Driver availability is a mediator of pricing policy, not a confounder; including it as a covariate blocks the causal pathway
No temporal split used despite strong temporal autocorrelation in ride demand patterns (rush hours, weekly cycles)
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split ignores endogeneity: historical prices affect observed demand and revenue, violating the i.i.d. assumption needed for observational evaluation
MSE on historical revenue predictions does not measure whether the dynamic pricing policy improves future revenue causally
Baseline (linear regression on historical averages) lacks access to real-time demand features, confounding model capacity with feature access
Driver availability is a mediator of pricing policy, not a confounder; including it as a covariate blocks the causal pathway
No temporal split used despite strong temporal autocorrelation in ride demand patterns (rush hours, weekly cycles)

critic_raw:
The article recommendation experiment using transformer embeddings versus cosine keyword similarity has a fundamental temporal validity problem. User click data from recommendation systems exhibits strong temporal non-stationarity: the distribution of popular articles, user preferences, and even the semantic content of abstracts shifts substantially over a 20-year corpus. A stratified random split treats 20-year-old clicks as exchangeable with recent clicks. This is not a reasonable assumption for a recommendation system meant to serve current users; a chronological split on user interaction timestamps is necessary to simulate deployment conditions.

CTR is chosen as the primary metric, but CTR in a logged dataset is subject to strong position bias and exposure bias: articles that appeared in prominent positions were more likely to be clicked regardless of genuine relevance. Training and evaluating on logged CTR without correcting for position bias means both models are primarily learning the historical exposure distribution rather than user preferences. Off-policy correction or counterfactual evaluation methods are required to obtain valid CTR estimates.

The baseline (keyword cosine similarity) is not given access to the same metadata and interaction logs as the transformer model. If the transformer model uses user history and behavioral signals, comparing it to a model using only keywords is not a fair evaluation of the representation method; it conflates feature engineering advantages with architectural ones. A fair comparison would give both models identical feature access.

The stratification rationale explicitly states it prioritizes 'static cross-sectional signal over temporal changes,' but this is precisely backward for a recommendation task: the business value lies in predicting which articles a user will engage with tomorrow, not in reconstructing the historical click distribution. This framing reveals a fundamental misalignment between the evaluation design and the deployment goal.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split over a 20-year corpus ignores temporal non-stationarity in user preferences and article relevance distributions
CTR from logged data reflects position and exposure bias, not genuine user preference; no off-policy correction is applied
Transformer model may access behavioral metadata unavailable to the keyword baseline, conflating feature advantage with architecture advantage
Evaluation design explicitly prioritizes cross-sectional signal over temporal dynamics, which is backward for deployment-oriented recommendation
No cold-start evaluation included, yet the hypothesis concerns improving recommendations for articles and users with sparse history
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split over a 20-year corpus ignores temporal non-stationarity in user preferences and article relevance distributions
CTR from logged data reflects position and exposure bias, not genuine user preference; no off-policy correction is applied
Transformer model may access behavioral metadata unavailable to the keyword baseline, conflating feature advantage with architecture advantage
Evaluation design explicitly prioritizes cross-sectional signal over temporal dynamics, which is backward for deployment-oriented recommendation
No cold-start evaluation included, yet the hypothesis concerns improving recommendations for articles and users with sparse history

critic_raw:
This is a notably rigorous experimental design for merchant churn prediction. The chronological split with per-merchant containment, warm-up period to avoid left-edge artifacts, and snapshot-based labeling definition are all methodologically sound. The exclusion of post-decision features (closure workflow status, retention-case outcomes) reflects genuine causal hygiene. PR-AUC as the primary metric is well-justified for the class-imbalanced, queue-prioritization use case, and the secondary diagnostics (Recall@K, calibration slope, precision at quota) are appropriately operationalized.

The most substantive issue is the comparison between model families that differ in both architecture and feature representation. The sequence model operates on raw event streams while the baseline operates on aggregated features. Even with the note that the baseline receives 'obvious retention signals,' this design cannot fully attribute performance differences to the sequence modeling capability versus richer raw feature access. A more diagnostic design would include an ablation: a tabular model trained on summary statistics derived directly from the event stream (not just monthly aggregates) to isolate the contribution of sequence order from representation richness.

The 90-day pre-snapshot window for the sequence model and the 'monthly and short-horizon' summaries for the baseline may not be aligned in terms of effective lookback. If the tabular baseline aggregates over 3 months to match the sequence window, this is fair; if aggregates are coarser (e.g., monthly buckets only), the comparison under-represents the baseline's potential. The design should explicitly document that both models see the same effective historical window.

Subgroup reporting by merchant tenure, acquisition channel, size, and seasonal period is planned, but no plan is specified for how performance differences in subgroups will be attributed or acted upon. This is a minor point—these are diagnostic, not confirmatory—but the design would benefit from specifying whether subgroup results are used only for interpretation or also for model selection.
all_issues_raised: Architecture and feature representation are conflated: sequence model uses raw events while baseline uses aggregates, making it impossible to isolate sequence modeling contribution alone
Lookback window alignment between sequence model (90-day event stream) and tabular baseline (monthly aggregates) is not explicitly verified as equivalent
No ablation proposed to separate the effect of raw event access from sequential ordering in the proposed model
Subgroup analysis purpose (diagnostic vs. model selection criterion) is unspecified, leaving its role in the experiment ambiguous
Snapshot containment per merchant is correct, but the design does not address merchants that churn and reactivate within the 24-month window
all_issues_adjudicated: Architecture and feature representation are conflated: sequence model uses raw events while baseline uses aggregates, making it impossible to isolate sequence modeling contribution alone
Lookback window alignment between sequence model (90-day event stream) and tabular baseline (monthly aggregates) is not explicitly verified as equivalent
No ablation proposed to separate the effect of raw event access from sequential ordering in the proposed model
Subgroup analysis purpose (diagnostic vs. model selection criterion) is unspecified, leaving its role in the experiment ambiguous
Snapshot containment per merchant is correct, but the design does not address merchants that churn and reactivate within the 24-month window

critic_raw:
The customer satisfaction prediction design from travel platform UGC has a critical metric choice problem. Accuracy is selected as the primary metric for a binary classification task in a domain likely exhibiting class imbalance (satisfied vs. dissatisfied customers may not be balanced). Accuracy is maximally uninformative under imbalance: a model predicting the majority class exclusively achieves high accuracy without any discriminative value. The design later notes F1, precision, and recall as secondaries, but designating accuracy as primary reveals a misunderstanding of the evaluation challenge.

The stratified random split is justified by asserting 'no strong temporal pattern underlies the sentiment data.' This is a strong assumption for a five-year travel platform dataset. Travel sentiment is known to follow temporal patterns: COVID-19-period reviews, seasonal demand spikes, platform policy changes, and major travel disruptions create distributional shifts. Asserting the signal is purely cross-sectional without empirical validation of stationarity is methodologically unjustified. The design should at minimum test this assumption (e.g., by comparing performance of a temporally-split model versus random-split to check for optimism inflation).

The baseline lexicon-based method is 'tuned to optimize lexicon choices and operational thresholds,' but lexicon-based sentiment systems often have the advantage of being interpretable and well-calibrated for general sentiment. Comparing a domain-fine-tuned BERT to a general-purpose lexicon without either fine-tuning the lexicon to the travel domain or using a domain-specific lexicon (e.g., SentiWordNet applied to hospitality vocabulary) is an unfair comparison that understates the baseline.

Exclusion of confounded periods (policy changes, regional events) is mentioned as a potential mitigation. However, excluding data to avoid confounds without a principled criterion risks introducing selection bias: if confounded periods are systematically excluded from the test set, the evaluation overstates performance on 'clean' data that does not reflect real deployment conditions.
all_issues_raised: Accuracy as primary metric is inappropriate for a likely imbalanced binary sentiment classification task
Stratified random split assumes temporal stationarity over five years without empirical validation, ignoring known travel sector temporal shocks
Lexicon baseline is not domain-adapted to the travel sector, understating its potential performance relative to the fine-tuned transformer
Excluding confounded time periods from analysis risks selection bias that inflates estimated performance beyond real deployment conditions
No calibration assessment is planned despite customer satisfaction prediction being used for personalization decisions that depend on probability estimates
all_issues_adjudicated: Accuracy as primary metric is inappropriate for a likely imbalanced binary sentiment classification task
Stratified random split assumes temporal stationarity over five years without empirical validation, ignoring known travel sector temporal shocks
Lexicon baseline is not domain-adapted to the travel sector, understating its potential performance relative to the fine-tuned transformer
Excluding confounded time periods from analysis risks selection bias that inflates estimated performance beyond real deployment conditions
No calibration assessment is planned despite customer satisfaction prediction being used for personalization decisions that depend on probability estimates

critic_raw:
The SDOH-augmented hospital readmission prediction design has a key comparison structure problem. The experiment pits gradient-boosted trees with SDOH features against logistic regression with only clinical features. This creates a two-way confound: the comparison simultaneously varies both model family (GBDT vs. logistic regression) and feature set (clinical + SDOH vs. clinical only). Any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to SDOH features alone—it may reflect the capacity advantage of GBDT over logistic regression on the same features. A proper experiment would include at minimum a third arm: GBDT trained on clinical features only, enabling a clean attribution of the SDOH contribution independent of model complexity.

AUC-ROC is the primary metric, which is appropriate for a ranking task. However, for the clinical decision-support context (identifying high-risk patients for readmission interventions), calibration is as important as discrimination: a model with high AUC but poor calibration produces unreliable risk scores for clinical thresholding. The design should include a calibration assessment (e.g., Brier score, calibration plots, reliability diagrams).

The stratified random split on five-year EHR data assumes temporal stationarity. Hospital readmission risk is affected by policy changes (DRG payment reform, discharge protocols, care coordination initiatives) that occur over five years. A random split mixes temporal periods in ways that can inflate generalization estimates if the training data contains information about later policy environments that the model implicitly encodes.

SDOH features (income, education, housing) are often sparsely recorded in EHRs and carry substantial missing data correlated with the outcome: sicker or more disadvantaged patients may have more incomplete records. The imputation strategy is described only as 'fit on training set,' without specifying the method or addressing the potential for differential missingness to bias SDOH estimates.
all_issues_raised: Comparison confounds model family (GBDT vs. logistic regression) with feature set (SDOH+ vs. clinical-only), making SDOH contribution unattributable
No calibration assessment planned despite clinical decision-support requiring well-calibrated probability estimates
Stratified random split over five-year EHR data ignores temporal policy changes affecting readmission risk distributions
SDOH imputation strategy is unspecified; differential missingness in SDOH fields correlated with outcome can bias attribution
No analysis of whether SDOH feature availability is uniform across the patient population or systematically missing for certain subgroups
all_issues_adjudicated: Comparison confounds model family (GBDT vs. logistic regression) with feature set (SDOH+ vs. clinical-only), making SDOH contribution unattributable
No calibration assessment planned despite clinical decision-support requiring well-calibrated probability estimates
Stratified random split over five-year EHR data ignores temporal policy changes affecting readmission risk distributions
SDOH imputation strategy is unspecified; differential missingness in SDOH fields correlated with outcome can bias attribution
No analysis of whether SDOH feature availability is uniform across the patient population or systematically missing for certain subgroups

critic_raw:
The reinforcement learning inventory optimization experiment has a fundamental methodological problem: RL is an online, sequential decision-making framework, but the evaluation is conducted on a static historical dataset using a stratified random split. This is incompatible. RL agents learn by interacting with an environment and receiving rewards; evaluating them on shuffled historical data breaks the sequential reward structure and prevents the agent from receiving meaningful feedback about restocking decisions' downstream consequences. Stratified random splitting of an RL problem strips the temporal dependencies that define the RL objective.

The Q-learning algorithm is selected without justification for the state and action space dimensionality of grocery inventory management. Tabular Q-learning assumes discrete, small state spaces; a grocery chain with many SKUs and continuous demand signals would require function approximation (e.g., DQN or actor-critic methods). The design does not specify how the state space is represented or whether it is feasible for tabular Q-learning. This is a critical implementation gap.

The comparison between Q-learning and a heuristic baseline is claimed to involve 'equivalent tuning efforts,' but heuristic systems are tuned by domain expert rule adjustment while Q-learning is tuned through training episode count, reward shaping, and discount factor selection. These are not commensurable. More critically, the heuristic baseline has the advantage of domain-expert knowledge embedded in its rules, which is a substantial implicit information advantage over an RL agent learning from scratch.

Stockout reduction over a 'three-month evaluation period' is cited as the primary metric, but RL policies can exhibit temporal non-stationarity during learning (performance may degrade before improving). Without specifying whether this is an online evaluation of the trained policy or a simulation-based offline evaluation, it is unclear whether the three months are used for training, evaluation, or both.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split is incompatible with RL: the temporal reward structure required for Q-learning is destroyed by shuffling historical data
Tabular Q-learning is specified without justification for the state space complexity of multi-SKU grocery inventory
Tuning effort equivalence between Q-learning and expert heuristics is not operationally comparable
Three-month evaluation period conflates training and evaluation phases without specifying whether the RL policy is evaluated online or via simulation
No reward function specification is provided; reward shaping choices in RL are a major source of evaluation variance and gaming
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split is incompatible with RL: the temporal reward structure required for Q-learning is destroyed by shuffling historical data
Tabular Q-learning is specified without justification for the state space complexity of multi-SKU grocery inventory
Tuning effort equivalence between Q-learning and expert heuristics is not operationally comparable
Three-month evaluation period conflates training and evaluation phases without specifying whether the RL policy is evaluated online or via simulation
No reward function specification is provided; reward shaping choices in RL are a major source of evaluation variance and gaming

critic_raw:
The fraud detection experiment linking financial news sentiment to transaction fraud has a conceptual validity problem: the mechanism by which financial news articles causally influence individual transaction-level fraud is not established. Financial news may correlate with macroeconomic cycles that also correlate with fraud rates, but this is an observational correlation, not a causal relationship. Using news embeddings as features for individual transaction fraud without a credible mechanism is likely to introduce spurious correlations that do not generalize—particularly because news coverage of fraud incidents can be contemporaneous with the fraud itself, creating a temporal leakage risk if news and transactions are matched by date without a strict lag.

The stratified random split on transaction data with temporal autocorrelation is a standard flaw: fraud patterns evolve over time (fraud rings adapt tactics, card security technology changes), and random shuffling of a three-year dataset allows the model to train on transactions from the same fraud campaigns as its test set. A chronological split is required. The design explicitly acknowledges this assumption ('temporal autocorrelation is weak') without empirical support.

The decision tree baseline is a weak comparator for a modern fraud detection context where gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM) is the standard tabular baseline. Outperforming a single decision tree with a transformer is not a meaningful contribution. A fair evaluation requires a well-tuned gradient boosted ensemble as the baseline, not a decision tree.

Accuracy combined with False Positive Rate as the primary metric is problematic. Accuracy under extreme class imbalance (fraud is typically 0.1-1% of transactions) rewards the trivial reject-all strategy. The combination with FPR does not resolve this; a model that predicts all transactions as legitimate achieves perfect FPR with useless recall. AUCPR or F-beta with appropriate beta weighting is the standard for imbalanced fraud detection.
all_issues_raised: Causal mechanism linking financial news articles to individual transaction-level fraud is unestablished; news-transaction matching by date risks contemporaneous leakage
Stratified random split on three-year transaction data ignores temporal evolution of fraud tactics and campaign structure
Decision tree baseline is substantially weaker than the standard gradient boosted ensemble baseline for tabular fraud detection
Accuracy combined with FPR is invalid as primary metric under extreme class imbalance; AUCPR or F-beta is required
No mention of handling concept drift in fraud patterns, which is the primary deployment challenge for fraud detection models
all_issues_adjudicated: Causal mechanism linking financial news articles to individual transaction-level fraud is unestablished; news-transaction matching by date risks contemporaneous leakage
Stratified random split on three-year transaction data ignores temporal evolution of fraud tactics and campaign structure
Decision tree baseline is substantially weaker than the standard gradient boosted ensemble baseline for tabular fraud detection
Accuracy combined with FPR is invalid as primary metric under extreme class imbalance; AUCPR or F-beta is required
No mention of handling concept drift in fraud patterns, which is the primary deployment challenge for fraud detection models

critic_raw:
The RNN versus logistic regression readmission prediction design has a patient-level leakage problem that is partially addressed but incompletely resolved. The design stratifies splits by hospital, which is a useful confound control, but does not explicitly state that all visits from the same patient are kept in the same split. If a patient has multiple admissions across the three-year window, their visits could appear in both training and test sets. Because the RNN is trained on visit sequences, having earlier visits from the same patient in training while later visits are in the test set allows the RNN to implicitly learn patient-specific patterns rather than generalizable risk factors.

The justification for a stratified random split rests on the claim that 'temporal autocorrelation effects introduced by hospital practices are weak.' This is asserted without evidence. Hospital readmission risk is strongly affected by temporal factors: discharge policy changes, care coordination program implementations, and seasonal illness patterns all create temporal autocorrelation. A random split that ignores these produces overly optimistic generalization estimates.

AUROC is the primary metric, which is reasonable for rank-ordering readmission risk. However, for clinical deployment, the operating point matters: AUROC aggregates performance across all thresholds, which is useful for method comparison but does not reflect performance at the specific sensitivity/specificity operating point a care team would use. Decision curves or calibration curves at clinically relevant thresholds would strengthen the evaluation.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'demographic and diagnostic attributes' while the RNN uses 'temporally encoded visit sequences.' These are different feature sets. A three-arm comparison—logistic regression on static features, logistic regression on temporal aggregates derived from the visit sequences, and RNN on sequences—would isolate the contribution of temporal modeling from simple feature richness.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level containment across splits is not explicitly specified; multi-admission patients may leak visit sequences between training and test
Temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Logistic regression baseline and RNN use different feature sets, conflating feature richness with architecture contribution
AUROC aggregates across thresholds; clinical deployment requires calibration and performance at specific operating points
Stratification by hospital controls for site effects but does not address temporal confounding from policy changes within hospitals over three years
all_issues_adjudicated: Patient-level containment across splits is not explicitly specified; multi-admission patients may leak visit sequences between training and test
Temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Logistic regression baseline and RNN use different feature sets, conflating feature richness with architecture contribution
AUROC aggregates across thresholds; clinical deployment requires calibration and performance at specific operating points
Stratification by hospital controls for site effects but does not address temporal confounding from policy changes within hospitals over three years

critic_raw:
The ensemble method equipment failure prediction experiment in oil and gas has a temporal validity problem that is fundamental to predictive maintenance. Equipment failure is a rare, time-ordered event with strong temporal autocorrelation: sensor readings preceding failure form evolving degradation signatures. A stratified random split ignores this temporal structure and allows the model to train on sensor readings temporally adjacent (or posterior) to test-set failure events. This is a form of data leakage specific to temporal failure modeling: the degradation signature before a failure will be split such that some of it falls in training and some in test.

The feature engineering strategy 'targets interactions and nonlinearities intrinsic to equipment failure dynamics,' but the design does not specify the feature window length, aggregation method, or how features are constructed from multi-variate sensor streams. For equipment failure prediction, feature engineering choices (rolling statistics window length, spectral features, threshold exceedance indicators) are major determinants of model performance and must be described to assess reproducibility and fairness.

The rule-based baseline is described as 'oversimplifying real-time complexities,' which is a characterization that pre-judges the comparison. Rule-based systems in oil and gas are often carefully engineered with domain knowledge and may include multi-sensor fusion logic. The design does not describe the specific rule set being compared, making it impossible to assess whether the baseline represents an appropriate domain standard or a deliberately weak straw man.

F1-score is the primary metric. For rare failure events, F1 is sensitive to the threshold choice. The design uses cross-validation on the validation set for 'calibration,' but does not specify whether the threshold is fixed or whether the same threshold is applied to both the ensemble model and the rule-based baseline. Threshold optimization post-hoc on the validation set and then reporting F1 on the test set is a form of indirect overfitting to the test distribution if the threshold is not independently derived.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporal failure data allows training on sensor readings adjacent to test-set failure events, creating degradation signature leakage
Feature engineering window lengths, aggregation methods, and multi-sensor fusion approach are unspecified, undermining reproducibility assessment
Rule-based baseline is not described concretely; characterizing it as oversimplified without specifying the rules creates a straw man risk
F1 threshold selection procedure is unspecified; post-hoc threshold optimization on validation for F1 reporting can indirectly overfit test distribution
No analysis of failure type heterogeneity across sites; ensemble performance may be driven by a subset of equipment types rather than generalizing across the fleet
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporal failure data allows training on sensor readings adjacent to test-set failure events, creating degradation signature leakage
Feature engineering window lengths, aggregation methods, and multi-sensor fusion approach are unspecified, undermining reproducibility assessment
Rule-based baseline is not described concretely; characterizing it as oversimplified without specifying the rules creates a straw man risk
F1 threshold selection procedure is unspecified; post-hoc threshold optimization on validation for F1 reporting can indirectly overfit test distribution
No analysis of failure type heterogeneity across sites; ensemble performance may be driven by a subset of equipment types rather than generalizing across the fleet

critic_raw:
The RNN-with-attention versus logistic regression readmission prediction design at the multi-site hospital level shares a structural problem common to this class of experiments: the stratified random split is justified by assuming temporal autocorrelation is 'sufficiently weak,' but no evidence for this is provided. Hospital readmission rates are affected by care coordination program rollouts, DRG policy changes, and seasonal illness patterns—all of which introduce temporal autocorrelation that would systematically bias a random split toward optimism.

Recall is selected as the primary metric, which is appropriate for the stated goal of identifying as many true readmissions as possible. However, recall without a precision constraint is operationally meaningless: a model that flags all patients for intervention achieves perfect recall at zero precision. The design should specify the operating recall target or use a metric that incorporates precision (F-beta or AUCPR). Using recall for model selection on the validation set could select a model that is trivially biased toward predicting the positive class.

The feature set for the logistic regression baseline includes 'demographic and hospital identifiers.' Hospital identifiers as features for a model intended to generalize across sites are problematic: the model can learn site-specific base rates rather than generalizable patient risk factors. In deployment, a new site would be an unseen identifier, causing the model to fall back on population averages rather than learned site patterns. This reduces external validity.

The design notes that the RNN uses 'attention mechanisms' but does not specify how attention is applied (self-attention across visits, attention over features within a visit, or cross-modal attention). The type and implementation of attention substantially affect what patterns the model can capture, and its description is essential for assessing the architectural claim over logistic regression.
all_issues_raised: Temporal autocorrelation in readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Recall as the sole primary metric without a precision constraint is operationally uninterpretable and selects toward trivially positive-biased models
Hospital identifiers as input features encode site-specific base rates that do not generalize to new deployment sites
Attention mechanism type and implementation are unspecified, making the architectural advantage claim non-verifiable
No patient-level split containment is described for multi-admission patients across the three-year EHR window
all_issues_adjudicated: Temporal autocorrelation in readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Recall as the sole primary metric without a precision constraint is operationally uninterpretable and selects toward trivially positive-biased models
Hospital identifiers as input features encode site-specific base rates that do not generalize to new deployment sites
Attention mechanism type and implementation are unspecified, making the architectural advantage claim non-verifiable
No patient-level split containment is described for multi-admission patients across the three-year EHR window

critic_raw:
The deep reinforcement learning inventory management experiment inherits the same fundamental incompatibility between RL and static offline evaluation that appears in similar designs. Stratified random splitting on historical inventory data breaks the sequential state-transition structure that DRL requires. In DRL for inventory management, the agent's policy determines ordering actions, which affect stock levels, which determine future state observations. A static historical dataset represents only the actions and outcomes of the previous (rule-based) policy; training DRL on these logged transitions without proper off-policy correction methods (such as importance sampling or offline RL algorithms like CQL or TD3+BC) will yield a policy that cannot reliably improve on the behavior policy.

The design acknowledges that the temporal autocorrelation assumption 'requires further validation,' which is an unusual admission. This implies the design is internally aware of this methodological weakness but proceeds without resolving it. For a method comparison that is the core of the experiment, building it on an acknowledged assumption that requires validation is a significant design flaw.

The DRL framework's stockout reduction performance is evaluated on a reserved test set, but it is unclear whether this evaluation involves actual sequential decision-making (simulated rollout on test-period data) or simply scoring the policy's recommendations against test-set ground truth. If the latter, the evaluation is still observational and subject to distribution shift from the historical behavior policy.

The rule-based baseline is 'tuned to manage similar inventory costs,' but the DRL agent's objective is stockout reduction, not cost equivalence. If the rule-based system is tuned for cost while the DRL is optimized for stockouts, the comparison confounds objective function alignment with model capability, making it impossible to determine whether observed differences reflect policy quality or objective mismatch.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split is incompatible with DRL's sequential decision structure; offline RL requires counterfactual correction methods
The design acknowledges temporal autocorrelation assumption requires validation but proceeds without resolving it
Test-set evaluation method is ambiguous: observational scoring vs. simulated sequential rollout produces qualitatively different validity
Rule-based baseline is tuned for cost while DRL is optimized for stockouts, creating objective function misalignment in the comparison
No dead-stock or overstock cost is included in the evaluation; reducing stockouts by massively over-ordering is a trivial and costly solution
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split is incompatible with DRL's sequential decision structure; offline RL requires counterfactual correction methods
The design acknowledges temporal autocorrelation assumption requires validation but proceeds without resolving it
Test-set evaluation method is ambiguous: observational scoring vs. simulated sequential rollout produces qualitatively different validity
Rule-based baseline is tuned for cost while DRL is optimized for stockouts, creating objective function misalignment in the comparison
No dead-stock or overstock cost is included in the evaluation; reducing stockouts by massively over-ordering is a trivial and costly solution

critic_raw:
This RNN-versus-logistic-regression readmission prediction experiment uses recall as the primary metric to minimize false negatives and identify high-risk patients. While the clinical motivation is appropriate, recall without a paired precision constraint allows model selection to trivially favor a classifier that predicts readmission for most patients. The design does not specify a minimum acceptable precision, nor does it use AUCPR, which would evaluate recall across a range of precision levels and avoid this gaming problem. The stated stakeholder emphasis on reducing readmissions is better served by AUCPR or a Recall@Precision threshold.

The stratified random split justification—that 'temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split'—is unsupported. Three years of EHR data spans policy changes, seasonal variation, and potential care pathway revisions. Without an empirical test of stationarity (e.g., comparing model performance when trained on early years and tested on later years), this assumption represents a risk of inflated generalization estimates.

The RNN is trained on 'time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits,' while the logistic regression uses 'aggregate demographic and clinical variables.' This asymmetry means any performance difference may reflect the information advantage of using detailed temporal sequences over simple aggregates, not the RNN's architectural suitability. Including a logistic regression or gradient boosted model trained on temporal aggregate features derived from the same sequences would enable attribution.

Patient-level containment in the splits is not addressed. If patients have multiple admissions within the three-year window and their visits appear in both training and test sets, the RNN can memorize patient-specific patterns rather than learning generalizable risk factors. This is a critical validity concern for the patient-level comparison.
all_issues_raised: Recall as primary metric without precision constraint or AUCPR allows trivial high-recall models to win model selection
Temporal stationarity assumed but not empirically tested over the three-year EHR window
Feature asymmetry between RNN (temporal sequences) and logistic regression (aggregates) confounds architecture advantage with feature richness
Patient-level containment in train/test splits is unaddressed for multi-admission patients
No calibration assessment despite the clinical application requiring well-calibrated risk probabilities for intervention prioritization
all_issues_adjudicated: Recall as primary metric without precision constraint or AUCPR allows trivial high-recall models to win model selection
Temporal stationarity assumed but not empirically tested over the three-year EHR window
Feature asymmetry between RNN (temporal sequences) and logistic regression (aggregates) confounds architecture advantage with feature richness
Patient-level containment in train/test splits is unaddressed for multi-admission patients
No calibration assessment despite the clinical application requiring well-calibrated risk probabilities for intervention prioritization

critic_raw:
The CNN-versus-linear-regression vehicle telemetry predictive maintenance experiment has a temporal validity problem that is particularly acute for vehicle fleets. Mechanical degradation follows a temporal trajectory: telemetry signals evolve as components approach failure. A stratified random split on five-year telemetry data allows the model to train on telemetry readings that are temporally adjacent to test-set readings from the same vehicle's degradation trajectory. This constitutes data leakage specific to the degradation curve: the model can learn to interpolate within a known degradation trajectory rather than extrapolate to future states of previously unseen vehicles.

The task is framed as regression (predicting 'maintenance needs'), but the outcome is not precisely defined. Is the target a continuous time-to-failure value, a binary maintenance-needed indicator within a window, or a probability score? MAE as the primary metric implies a continuous regression target, but 'predicting necessity for maintenance before failures occur' sounds like a classification or survival analysis task. This ambiguity undermines the entire evaluation framework.

The linear regression baseline is an extremely weak comparator for a CNN-based deep model on time-series telemetry. A defensible baseline would be a gradient boosted model trained on hand-engineered rolling statistics from the same telemetry signals, or at minimum a ridge regression with polynomial features. Outperforming a simple linear regression does not establish CNN superiority; it establishes that nonlinear modeling improves over linear modeling, which is expected.

Vehicle-level containment in the splits is not addressed. If readings from the same vehicle appear in both training and test, the model can learn vehicle-specific behavioral signatures rather than generalizable failure patterns. For a fleet deployment context, the model should be validated on vehicles entirely held out from training.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporal telemetry data allows training on readings adjacent to test-set readings from the same vehicle's degradation trajectory
The regression target ('maintenance needs') is ambiguously defined; MAE is inconsistent with a classification or survival framing of the task
Linear regression is an excessively weak baseline; gradient boosted models on rolling statistics are the appropriate comparator
Vehicle-level containment in train/test splits is unaddressed; same-vehicle readings in both sets allow memorization of vehicle-specific signatures
No lead time analysis is included; maintenance value depends critically on how far in advance failures are predicted, not just predictive error magnitude
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporal telemetry data allows training on readings adjacent to test-set readings from the same vehicle's degradation trajectory
The regression target ('maintenance needs') is ambiguously defined; MAE is inconsistent with a classification or survival framing of the task
Linear regression is an excessively weak baseline; gradient boosted models on rolling statistics are the appropriate comparator
Vehicle-level containment in train/test splits is unaddressed; same-vehicle readings in both sets allow memorization of vehicle-specific signatures
No lead time analysis is included; maintenance value depends critically on how far in advance failures are predicted, not just predictive error magnitude

critic_raw:
The TCN versus LSTM deterioration prediction experiment in ICUs is notable for explicitly acknowledging the temporal split debate—'critics may argue that temporal dependencies should guide data partitioning'—without resolving it in the experimental design. ICU patient deterioration is strongly temporally structured: vital sign trajectories, nursing note language, and illness severity scoring all exhibit strong autocorrelation within and across patient stays. A stratified random split that interleaves ICU encounters from different time periods in training and test allows the model to be informed by physiological pattern norms from the same temporal period as test patients, inflating estimated AUC.

The comparison between TCN and LSTM is presented as a straightforward architecture comparison. However, TCNs and LSTMs have different inductive biases (parallel convolutions vs. recurrent state propagation) and different hyperparameter surfaces. Equal tuning effort does not mean equal search adequacy: if the LSTM is sensitive to learning rate in a way the TCN is not, a fixed search budget will disadvantage one architecture non-uniformly. The design should specify the search space and procedure for each architecture.

Nursing notes are mentioned as a data source but are not addressed in the feature engineering description. Nursing notes are unstructured text requiring NLP preprocessing. Whether they are included as bag-of-words features, embedded with a language model, or excluded entirely from the temporal sequence input is unspecified—yet this is likely a major driver of performance given the clinical richness of nursing documentation.

AUC is the primary metric. For ICU deterioration prediction, the clinical value is in the early warning alarm rate: how often does the model fire, and how many of those alarms are actionable? AUC does not capture alarm specificity. The design should include a metric tied to the alarm rate at a clinically defensible sensitivity level.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split is acknowledged as potentially problematic for temporal ICU data but is used anyway without resolution
TCN and LSTM hyperparameter search adequacy is not specified; fixed budget with different sensitivity surfaces disadvantages one architecture non-uniformly
Nursing notes are listed as a data source but their preprocessing and inclusion in the temporal model input is unspecified
AUC does not capture alarm rate or early warning lead time, which are the clinically relevant metrics for ICU deterioration prediction
Patient-level containment across splits is unspecified for patients with multi-day ICU stays spanning across the three-year data window
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split is acknowledged as potentially problematic for temporal ICU data but is used anyway without resolution
TCN and LSTM hyperparameter search adequacy is not specified; fixed budget with different sensitivity surfaces disadvantages one architecture non-uniformly
Nursing notes are listed as a data source but their preprocessing and inclusion in the temporal model input is unspecified
AUC does not capture alarm rate or early warning lead time, which are the clinically relevant metrics for ICU deterioration prediction
Patient-level containment across splits is unspecified for patients with multi-day ICU stays spanning across the three-year data window

critic_raw:
The wearable device neural network versus logistic regression readmission prediction experiment faces an acute data availability problem that is not addressed: wearable device data covering the 30 days post-discharge is only available if the patient wears the device continuously after discharge. The design does not address what fraction of patients have sufficient post-discharge wearable data, whether non-compliance is correlated with readmission risk (sicker patients may be less likely to wear devices), and how missingness in the wearable stream is handled. If compliance correlates with health status, the study population is systematically selected against the highest-risk patients, undermining the external validity of the neural network's advantage.

Only 10,000 patient records over one year constitutes a small dataset for training a neural network on time-series wearable data. The neural network's advantage over logistic regression on tabular data is typically only realized at scale; with 10,000 examples, logistic regression often matches or outperforms neural networks because the neural network's capacity cannot be effectively utilized without sufficient training examples. The design should address sample size adequacy for both models.

AUROC is the primary metric, but wearable data generates sequences of varying length (different devices, different wear compliance). The design mentions 'aggregation of statistics over specified windows' for feature engineering, but does not specify the window size, aggregation method, or how shorter-than-window records are handled. These choices substantially affect what information the neural network has access to and whether the comparison is fair.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'clinical demographics' while the neural network uses wearable device data plus demographics. Again, this confounds data modality access with architectural choice. A fairer comparison would include logistic regression on the same derived wearable features, enabling clean attribution to the neural network's capacity rather than its access to richer data.
all_issues_raised: Wearable device compliance post-discharge is likely correlated with readmission risk, creating selection bias that undermines external validity
10,000 records over one year may be insufficient for a neural network to demonstrate reliable advantage over logistic regression on tabular features
Window length, aggregation method, and handling of short-duration wearable records are unspecified, affecting reproducibility and fairness
Baseline (logistic regression on demographics) lacks access to wearable features, confounding data modality advantage with architecture advantage
No analysis of data completeness or compliance rates is planned, leaving the representativeness of the study population unknown
all_issues_adjudicated: Wearable device compliance post-discharge is likely correlated with readmission risk, creating selection bias that undermines external validity
10,000 records over one year may be insufficient for a neural network to demonstrate reliable advantage over logistic regression on tabular features
Window length, aggregation method, and handling of short-duration wearable records are unspecified, affecting reproducibility and fairness
Baseline (logistic regression on demographics) lacks access to wearable features, confounding data modality advantage with architecture advantage
No analysis of data completeness or compliance rates is planned, leaving the representativeness of the study population unknown

critic_raw:
The unsupervised anomaly detection experiment using an autoencoder for industrial IoT fault detection has a fundamental conceptual inconsistency. The model is described as 'unsupervised,' but the evaluation uses labeled fault events from maintenance logs as ground truth, and the validation procedure uses these labels to tune hyperparameters. A truly unsupervised model should not have access to labeled fault events during training—the unsupervised property means the model learns a reconstruction distribution from normal data only. However, model selection on the validation set using labeled fault events means the anomaly threshold (the key hyperparameter) is supervised by fault labels. This is semi-supervised at best and must be acknowledged as such.

Stratified random split on temporal IoT sensor data suffers from the same degradation-signature leakage problem as other predictive maintenance designs. Equipment degradation follows a temporal trajectory; random shuffling allows training samples from the late degradation phase (close to failure) to end up in training while their temporal neighbors are in test. The autoencoder's reconstruction error on test samples near failure may thus be inflated by having seen similar degradation-phase data in training.

Mean time to failure (MTTF) as the primary metric is unusual for an anomaly detection evaluation. MTTF is typically a reliability engineering metric describing the expected operational lifetime of a system, not a model performance metric. The design appears to use it as 'average lead time between anomaly detection and actual failure,' which is a different and non-standard use. This metric's computation procedure is not specified and would require knowing the timing of both anomaly alerts and subsequent confirmed failures, which depends on the alert threshold.

The comparison is claimed to be 'fair as both methods undergo equivalent tuning,' but threshold-based systems have a single tunable parameter (the threshold), while autoencoders have architecture choices (depth, bottleneck size), reconstruction loss, and the anomaly threshold. These are not comparable tuning surfaces.
all_issues_raised: Model is described as unsupervised but validation uses fault labels to tune the anomaly threshold, making the approach semi-supervised
Stratified random split on temporal sensor data creates degradation-signature leakage for near-failure samples
MTTF is used as a model performance metric but is a reliability engineering term; the computation procedure is not specified
Tuning surfaces between autoencoder (architecture + threshold) and threshold-based system (threshold only) are incomparable; fairness claim is unsupported
No specification of what constitutes 'normal' training data for the unsupervised autoencoder; if faults contaminate normal training data, the reconstruction baseline is corrupted
all_issues_adjudicated: Model is described as unsupervised but validation uses fault labels to tune the anomaly threshold, making the approach semi-supervised
Stratified random split on temporal sensor data creates degradation-signature leakage for near-failure samples
MTTF is used as a model performance metric but is a reliability engineering term; the computation procedure is not specified
Tuning surfaces between autoencoder (architecture + threshold) and threshold-based system (threshold only) are incomparable; fairness claim is unsupported
No specification of what constitutes 'normal' training data for the unsupervised autoencoder; if faults contaminate normal training data, the reconstruction baseline is corrupted

critic_raw:
The student dropout prediction experiment using an NLP model on interaction logs has a temporal structure problem with meaningful operational implications. Student engagement data has strong temporal autocorrelation within a course: early engagement patterns predict late engagement, which predicts dropout. A stratified random split interleaves interaction records from different time periods in the same course, allowing the model to train on later engagement data from the same students whose earlier records appear in the test set. For an online learning platform where the goal is early identification of at-risk students, a chronological split (or course-enrollment-level containment) is necessary to simulate the realistic prediction scenario.

Oversampling dropout instances in training data is implemented without specifying the method (SMOTE, random oversampling, class-weighted loss) or whether oversampling is applied before or after the train/validation/test split. If oversampling is applied to the full dataset before splitting, synthetic instances from dropout students in the test set may appear in training, constituting leakage. The design must confirm oversampling occurs only within the training fold.

The BERT-based NLP model 'synthesizes interaction text data and engineered metrics.' If the interaction text includes message timestamps, forum post dates, or course-relative timing information, these provide direct temporal signals that are not available to the logistic regression baseline (which uses only demographics). This asymmetry in temporal feature access is a significant confound that the design does not address.

The design acknowledges that exogenous factors such as course revisions 'might not account for all such possible confounds.' However, for a platform spanning several academic years, course revisions are not minor: they restructure the interaction features themselves. A student's interaction with a course after revision may have a fundamentally different relationship to dropout compared to pre-revision interactions, making cross-temporal comparisons unreliable without course-version controls.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on temporally ordered course interaction data allows training on later engagement from the same students as test-set early engagement records
Oversampling procedure is unspecified; if applied before splitting, synthetic dropout instances can leak between folds
BERT model has access to interaction text with implicit temporal signals not available to the demographic-only logistic regression baseline
Course revision events across several academic years create structural breaks in the feature-to-dropout relationship that are not controlled for
AUPRC is the primary metric but the design selects the model on validation AUPRC without specifying the operating threshold used for early warning delivery
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporally ordered course interaction data allows training on later engagement from the same students as test-set early engagement records
Oversampling procedure is unspecified; if applied before splitting, synthetic dropout instances can leak between folds
BERT model has access to interaction text with implicit temporal signals not available to the demographic-only logistic regression baseline
Course revision events across several academic years create structural breaks in the feature-to-dropout relationship that are not controlled for
AUPRC is the primary metric but the design selects the model on validation AUPRC without specifying the operating threshold used for early warning delivery
